retrieved_docs.title: Migraine With Aura -- Treatment / Management -- Calcitonin gene-related peptide antagonists
retrieved_docs.content: Small molecule antagonists, or gepants, are oral agents proven effective for acute migraine treatment. Though more commonly used as a prophylactic therapy, some formulations are effective for abortive use. Rimegepant, administered as a single 75 mg dose, and ubrogepant are among the available options. CGRP antagonists are helpful for patients who do not respond to conventional therapy or those with coronary artery disease. [26] Rimegepant has approval for both acute and preventative treatment. [27] Zavegepant offers an intranasal option.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, eptinezumab, galcanezumab, fremanezumab) are used for migraine prophylaxis in episodic and chronic migraine. Rimegepant and ubrogepant are used for acute treatment of migraine with or without aura. galcanezumab and fremanezumab are under development for the preventive treatment of cluster headaches.

retrieved_docs.content: Studies indicate that a trial of 3 to 6 months may be necessary to evaluate the full effect of some preventative medications. Avoid valproate in women of childbearing age and topiramate during pregnancy.

retrieved_docs.content: Pharmacologic modulation of CGRP activity appears to mediate trigeminovascular pain transmission in migraine. CGRP antagonists are novel options for acute migraine treatment in patients with either insufficient response or contraindications to first-line medications. [17] [18] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Ubrogepant is given orally at a dosage of 50 mg or 100 mg. 2 hours after the initial dose, a second dose may be taken if necessary. The dose should not exceed 200 mg in 24 hours. Treating any more than eight migraine episodes in 30 days is not recommended, as its safety has not yet been established. Like rimegepant, ubrogepant is for the acute treatment of migraine with or without aura. [16] Atogepant is given orally for the prevention of episodic or chronic migraines at doses of 10 mg, 30 mg, or 60 mg daily, with or without food. Zavegepant is given intranasally at 10 mg per spray once daily.

retrieved_docs.title: Migraine Headache -- Treatment / Management -- Prophylactic or Preventive Treatments
retrieved_docs.content: Preventive treatment aims to reduce migraine frequency, improve responsiveness to the severity and duration of acute attacks, and reduce disability. [63] [64] Patients should document their migraine triggers to effectively reduce their impact in the future. Indications for preventive treatment include: Frequent or long-lasting headaches Attacks causing significant disability and reduced quality of life Contraindications or failure with acute therapies Significant adverse effects from abortive therapies Risk of medication overuse headache Menstrual migraine (with short-term premenstrual prophylaxis) Hemiplegic migraine Brainstem aura migraine Persistent aura without infarction Migrainous infarction

retrieved_docs.content: CGRP antagonists are reasonable options for patients who are unresponsive to the above options. They are available in parenteral, large molecules monoclonal antibodies targeting the CGRP receptor and oral small-molecule antagonists. Eptinezumab dosed 100 to 300 mg IV infusion once every 3 months Fremanezumab dosed 225 mg subcutaneously once monthly or 675 mg once every 3 months Atogepant 10, 30, or 60 mg orally once daily Rimegepant 75 mg orally every other day for prevention and 75 mg once daily for acute treatment

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Calcitonin gene-related peptide receptors (CGRP) receptor antagonists and monoclonal antibodies against CGRP and its receptor are medications used in the management and treatment of migraine, which is the sixth-highest cause worldwide of years lost due to disability as per the Global Burden of Disease Study (2013). This activity describes the indications, actions, and contraindications for these drugs as valuable agents in the prophylaxis and acute management of migraine and cluster headaches. This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, monitoring, adverse events, relevant interactions, and other key elements in the clinical setting as relates to the essential points needed by members of an interprofessional team managing the care of patients with migraines.

retrieved_docs.content: Adding dexamethasone to the treatment regimen for patients receiving the above therapies is recommended to reduce the risk of early headache recurrence. This adjunctive therapy can help improve the overall efficacy of migraine management in the emergency room setting.

retrieved_docs.title: Migraine Headache -- Treatment / Management -- Acute or Abortive Treatments
retrieved_docs.content: Antiemetics: Anti-nausea medications such as metoclopramide, chlorpromazine, or prochlorperazine are commonly used adjunctively with NSAIDs or triptans to reduce nausea and vomiting, particularly in emergency settings. Diphenhydramine may also be added to prevent dystonic reactions, primarily associated with metoclopramide.

retrieved_docs.title: Zolmitriptan -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Zolmitriptan has been found to have off-label uses for the following purposes: Acute treatment of cluster headaches when administered as a nasal spray, as endorsed by a Level A recommendation from the American Academy of Neurology (AAN). [2] Managing menstrual migraines. [3] Acute migraine headaches and related symptoms include nausea, vomiting, phonophobia, and photophobia. [1] [4] Zolmitriptan showed significant efficacy in helping patients attain pain-free status at 2 hours, 3 hours, and 4 hours following the treatment, compared to the placebo group. [5] [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Almotriptan -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the indications for almotriptan use. Differentiate between various migraine types and accurately diagnose those suitable for almotriptan intervention, considering factors such as aura, frequency, and associated symptoms. Implement evidence-based guidelines in the use of almotriptan for acute migraine attacks, including dosage adjustments and appropriate timing of administration. Collaborate with the interprofessional team to coordinate a comprehensive approach to migraine management, especially in cases requiring a multidisciplinary perspective. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 361
retrieved_docs.content: Antimigraine preparations constitute a therapeutic class that includes a diverse range of agents used to treat migraine attacks. Within this class are the oral‑dose formulation of clonidine marketed as “Clonidine only product in oral dose form,” the broader category of “Other antimigraine preparations in ATC,” and the specific triptan almotriptan. Additional members of the antimigraine preparations class are atogepant, dichloralphenazone, flunarizin‑dihydrochlorid, lisurid, methysergid, and naratriptan. All of these compounds share the common purpose of alleviating migraine symptoms, either through acute relief or preventive mechanisms, and are therefore grouped together under the umbrella of antimigraine preparations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 173
retrieved_docs.content: are linked to ketoconazole’s inhibition of cytochrome P450 enzymes; concurrent use with ergotamine derivatives (dihydroergotamine mesylate, dihydroergotamine tartrate), the vasopressin‑V2 antagonist conivaptan hydrochloride, or the acute migraine medication ubrogepant is contraindicated because of the risk of adverse pharmacologic interactions. Awareness of these contraindications, together with the detailed composition of each medicated shampoo, is essential for safe selection of topical therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 891
retrieved_docs.content: Antimigraine preparations comprise a diverse group of medications used to prevent or treat migraine attacks. This category includes the oral‑dose product of clonidine, other antimigraine agents classified in the ATC system, and specific drugs such as almotriptan, naratriptan, methysergid, atogepant, dichloralphenazone, lisurid, and pizotylin. These agents vary in their mechanisms of action—triptans like almotriptan, naratriptan, and methysergid act as serotonin receptor agonists, atogepant is a CGRP receptor antagonist, dichloralphenazone provides analgesic and sedative effects, and clonidine, lisurid, and pizotylin have additional vasoconstrictive or neuromodulatory properties. Together, they offer clinicians multiple therapeutic options for individualized migraine management and improve patient care by targeting different pathophysiological pathways involved in migraine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 781
retrieved_docs.content: Clonidine only product in oral dose form, Other antimigraine preparations in ATC, almotriptan, atogepant, dichloralphenazone, flunarizin‑dihydrochlorid, lisurid, methysergid, naratriptan and pizotylin are all classified as antimigraine preparations. This therapeutic class groups together a diverse range of agents that are used to prevent or treat migraine attacks, despite differences in their chemical structures and mechanisms of action. Recognizing these drugs as members of the same antimigraine preparation category helps clinicians select appropriate migraine therapy and understand that each of these agents shares the common purpose of alleviating migraine symptoms.

Moreover, the migraine medication ubrogepant is contraindicated in the presence of ketoconazole, indicating that ketoconazole can affect the safety or efficacy of ubrogepant. These contraindication relationships underscore the importance of reviewing a patient’s medication list for ergotamine‑type agents, vasopressin antagonists, and certain CGRP receptor antagonists before initiating ketoconazole‑containing topical therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 643
retrieved_docs.content: Antimigraine preparations comprise a diverse group of pharmacologic agents used to treat migraine, including clonidine formulated for oral dosing, the broader category of other ATC‑classified antimigraine drugs, and specific compounds such as almotriptan, atogepant, dichloralphenazone, flunarizin‑dihydrochloride, lisuride, methysergide, naratriptan, and the alkaloid subclass námelové alkaloidy. Pizotylin is also classified within this therapeutic class. Together, these agents represent various mechanisms of action—triptans (almotriptan, naratriptan), CGRP receptor antagonists (atogepant), serotonin‑related agents (lisuride, methysergide), and other migraine‑targeted compounds—providing clinicians with multiple options for individualized migraine management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 1578
retrieved_docs.content: Antimigraine preparations encompass a diverse group of pharmacologic agents used to prevent or treat migraine attacks. Among these agents are pizotylin, atogepant, dichloralphenazone, flunarizin‑dihydrochloride, lisurid, and methysergid, each classified as an antimigraine preparation. Although these compounds belong to different chemical classes and have distinct mechanisms of action, they share the common therapeutic goal of alleviating migraine symptoms. Recognizing that all of these medications are part of the broader category of antimigraine preparations assists clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy based on patient‑specific factors and drug characteristics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 334
retrieved_docs.content: ketoconazole is contraindicated with ubrogepant, a calcitonin gene‑related peptide (CGRP) receptor antagonist used for migraine treatment. These contraindications underscore the need for careful medication reconciliation when prescribing ketoconazole‑containing shampoos to patients who are receiving ergotamine derivatives or ubrogepant. Overall, ketoconazole’s role as a potent antifungal agent is reflected in its widespread inclusion in medicated shampoos, while its interaction profile mandates vigilance to avoid adverse effects in patients taking specific migraine and ergot‑based therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 68
retrieved_docs.content: Clonidine only product in oral dose form, other antimigraine preparations in ATC, almotriptan, atogepant, dichloralphenazone, flunarizin‑dihydrochlorid, lisurid, methysergid, and naratriptan are all classified as antimigraine preparations, representing a diverse group of pharmacologic agents used to prevent or treat migraine attacks. Within this therapeutic class, the subgroup known as “námelové alkaloidy” is identified as a child category, indicating that certain alkaloid‑derived compounds also belong to the broader antimigraine preparations family. By grouping these varied medications under the single umbrella of antimigraine preparations, clinicians can recognize their shared therapeutic purpose while also appreciating the distinct mechanisms and chemical structures—ranging from triptans (almotriptan, naratriptan) to gepants (atogepant) and other agents such as dichloralphenazone and flunarizin‑dihydrochlorid—that contribute to migraine management. This integrated classification supports comprehensive patient care by facilitating selection of appropriate agents based on individual response, side‑effect profiles, and specific migraine characteristics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1202
retrieved_docs.content: Clonidine, identified as the only product available in an oral dose form, and a group labeled “Other antimigraine preparations in ATC” are both classified as antimigraine preparations. The broader category of antimigraine preparations also encompasses a variety of specific agents, including atogepant, dichloralphenazone, flunarizin‑dihydrochloride, lisuride, methysergide, and naratriptan. All of these compounds are recognized as distinct types of antimigraine preparations, reflecting the diverse pharmacologic options available for the prevention and treatment of migraine attacks. This classification highlights the range of therapeutic choices within the antimigraine drug class, supporting tailored patient care based on individual response and safety considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1870
retrieved_docs.content: Icatibant is classified among the drugs used to treat hereditary angioedema, a rare genetic disorder characterized by episodic swelling of the skin and mucosal tissues. As a bradykinin B2‑receptor antagonist, icatibant specifically blocks the action of bradykinin, a key mediator of the swelling attacks in hereditary angioedema, thereby providing rapid relief of symptoms during acute episodes. Inclusion of icatibant in the therapeutic armamentarium underscores its role as a targeted pharmacologic option for managing this condition and improving patient outcomes.

The class is also linked to the neuropeptide galanin, suggesting possible pharmacological interactions. Clinical caution is required because homatropine methylbromide and butorfanol are contraindicated in patients with respiratory depression, highlighting the need for careful assessment when anticholinergic agents affect respiratory function. Finally, CGRP receptor antagonists are mentioned as another receptor‑antagonist class relevant to migraine treatment, illustrating the wider context of receptor‑blocking therapeutics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 563
retrieved_docs.content: Ecallantide and icatibant are pharmacologic agents that belong to the therapeutic class of drugs used in hereditary angioedema. Both medications are specifically indicated for the management of hereditary angioedema attacks, targeting the underlying mechanisms of the disorder to reduce swelling and improve patient outcomes. Their inclusion in this drug class highlights their shared role in treating this rare, potentially life‑threatening condition.

Conivaptan hydrochloride is also contraindicated with ketoconazole, and ketoconazole itself is contraindicated with ubrogepant. These contraindications underscore the need for clinicians to review a patient’s medication regimen before prescribing ketoconazole‑containing products, particularly when the patient is receiving ergotamine derivatives, vasopressin antagonists, or CGRP receptor antagonists.
